pairs.question: What was the total number of B-17 Flying Fortresses produced during World War II?
pairs.answer: A total of 12,677 production B-17 Flying Fortresses were built before production ended.

pairs.question: Which variant of the B-17 was converted for search and rescue missions and how was it modified?
pairs.answer: The B-17H (later redesignated SB-17G postwar) was converted for search and rescue missions. It was modified to carry a lifeboat under the fuselage for recovery operations.

pairs.question: When was the last operational B-17 mission flown by the U.S. Air Force?
pairs.answer: The last operational USAF B-17 mission occurred on 6 August 1959, when a DB-17P directed a QB-17G as a target for an AIM-4 Falcon missile fired from an F-101 Voodoo near Holloman AFB, New Mexico.

pairs.question: How many overseas combat groups were equipped with the B-17 in August 1944?
pairs.answer: In August 1944, the Boeing B-17 equipped no less than 33 overseas combat groups.

pairs.question: What happened to most B-17s after World War II ended?
pairs.answer: Following the end of World War II, most Fortresses were flown to disposal units. The majority were cut up for scrap, while a few were sold to private owners or repurposed for other uses.

pairs.question: Provide a Python code snippet that calculates the operational lifespan of the B-17 from its first delivery to the last operational mission.
pairs.answer:
python
# Operational lifespan calculation for the B-17 Flying Fortress
first_delivery_date = (1936, 1, 11)  # Y1B-17 Fortress delivered on 11 January 1936
last_mission_date = (1959, 8, 6)     # Last operational mission on 6 August 1959

# Calculate the difference in years
operational_years = last_mission_date[0] - first_delivery_date[0]
print(f"The B-17 had an operational lifespan of approximately {operational_years} years.")

pairs.question: What were some of the postwar roles of the B-17 after its combat service?
pairs.answer: Postwar, the B-17 served multiple roles including search and rescue (SB-17G), air photogrammetry (RB-17G for the APCS), and as drone controllers (DB-17P) or drones (QB-17G). Some were also transferred to museums after retirement.
